segments.s302-g65: بر این بر نهادند is a past-tense clause: نهادند (they placed/settled) is the verb and بر این (upon this) is the prepositional phrase marking what they settled on. The repeated بر is a poetic doubling emphasizing location/target. یکسر is an adverbial meaning "entirely/at once," and سخن (object) here means "a single speech/opinion" — together the phrase means they unanimously settled on this one opinion.
segments.s302-g66: This negative clause expands the previous idea: کسی (in the negative context = "no one") is the subject, رای "opinion" is the noun, دیگر "other/another" modifies it, and نیفگند is an older-form negative past (literally “did not cast/throw”) meaning "did not put forward". بن at the end is an enclitic/poetic particle here whose semantic role is not matched in the provided sense list (hence flagged below).
segments.s302-g67: برفتند is the past plural verb "they went/proceeded" (the subject is omitted and expressed by the verb ending). یکسر is an adverb modifying the verb meaning "all at once/entirely," and به گلزریون is a prepositional phrase giving the destination (Golzariyun, a place name).
segments.s302-g68: همه ("all/everyone") scopes over the two coordinated nominal phrases. دیده پر آب literally "eye full of water" uses پر + noun to mean "full (of)," so "eyes full of tears," and دل پر ز خون uses the preposition ز (poetic "from/because of") to express "hearts full of blood" (i.e. full with blood/bloodshed).
segments.s302-g69: This is a locative/time description: به گلزریون marks the place, شاه توران is the subject phrase "the King of Turan," and سه روز gives the duration "three days." The verb appears in the next segment (ببود).
segments.s302-g70: ببود is an archaic or poetic past form meaning "was/remained." It is coordinated with برآسود ("was at ease/relaxed/took rest"). با introduces the instruments/companions of leisure: باز (here the hunting bird "falcon/hawk") and یوز (a hunting animal, "cheetah/hound used in hunting").
segments.s302-g71: برفتند (they went) is the verb; زان جایگه is a prepositional/locative phrase meaning "from that place/spot" (زان = "from that"); سوی گنگ means "toward Gank" (سوی + place). The phrase describes motion away from the previously mentioned location toward the place called گنگ.
segments.s302-g72: This is a negative-existential clause: به جایی نبودش literally "there was not any place for it" — نبودش (was not) takes the clitic -ش referring back to the possibility of a place to delay. فراوان modifies درنگ to mean "much/abundant delay," so the whole means "there was no place for much delay" (i.e. they could not tarry).
segments.s302-g25: The prepositional phrase بر این رزمگاه (literally 'on/upon this battlefield') is fronted to set the location. آفرین باد is an exclamatory formula: آفرین = an acclamation ('praise'/'bravo') and باد is the optative particle meaning 'may (it be)'; گفت is the past narrative verb 'said' (introducing the quoted acclamation). The sequence therefore means 'upon this battlefield (they) said “May there be praise.”'
segments.s302-g26: همه‌ساله is an adverb meaning 'yearly/each year' that modifies the action which follows; با اختر نیک جفت is a prepositional/nominal phrase 'paired with an auspicious star' (با = 'with', اختر = 'star', نیک = 'auspicious', جفت = 'pair'). The whole segment comments that this acclamation or rite was yearly associated with a favorable star.
segments.s302-g27: نهادند is the past plural (or narrative) form of 'to place/set' ('they placed/affixed'). بر نامه is 'on the letter/document' (بر = on, نامه = letter). مهری ز مشک is an object phrase meaning 'a (mahrī) of musk' — here مهری functions as a seal/mark associated with affection and ز مشک 'made of/from musk' specifies the material/scent.
segments.s302-g28: از آن پس is a temporal phrase 'after that' (از = from, آن = then/that, پس = after). گذر کرد is a compound where گذر (noun 'passage') is combined with کرد (past of 'to do/make') to serve as a verbal expression 'passed' (here the lexical list lacks the verbal sense for گذر so the meaning is given in context). بر ریگ خشک = 'over the dry sand' (بر = over/on, ریگ = sand, خشک = dry).
segments.s302-g29: چو here functions as a temporal conjunction 'when'. ز آن رود = 'from/at that river' (ز = from/of, آن = that, رود = river), and جیحون names the river (Jihun/Amu Darya). شد after a noun phrase like this is the past auxiliary/verb 'became/arrived' in narrative use; with the following name it indicates 'Afrasiab came to/was at that river.'
segments.s302-g30: This clause describes the motion of the water with a simile: چو باد = 'like the wind' (چو = as/like, باد = wind), دمان = 'edge/hem' (here indicating the bank or margin), تیز = 'swift', and بگذشت (past of گذشت) = 'passed/ran by'; آب = 'water' is the subject/agent. Word order is poetic: the simile and manner (like the wind, swift) precede the verb bگذشت 'passed'.
segments.s302-g31: به پیش is a locative/phrase meaning 'before/in front of' (به = to/at, پیش = in the presence of). سپاه قراخان = 'the army of Qara Khan' (سپاه = army, قراخان = title/name). رسید is the past tense 'arrived/reached'; together the clause means 'he arrived before the army of Qara Khan.'
segments.s302-g32: همی گفت is a narrative phrase often meaning 'he/they kept saying' or 'then said' (همی marks continuous or habitual aspect in classical Persian, گفت is past 'said' — note the provided lemma senses do not include the verbal sense so the index for گفت is not available). هر کس ز جنگ آنچه دید is a free relative 'each person (told) whatever he had seen of the battle' (هر کس = every person, ز جنگ = concerning the battle, آنچه دید = what he saw). The full clause reports ongoing speech of witnesses describing the battle.
segments.s302-g33: سپهدار ترکان is a genitive/nominal phrase 'commander of the Turks' (noun + possessor). چه مایه is an exclamatory measure 'how much / to what extent', and گریست is the simple past 3rd‑singular of گریستن 'to weep' — together: 'how much the commander of the Turks wept'.
segments.s302-g34: بر here is the preposition 'over/for' (as in 'wept for somebody'); آن کس 'that person'; که introduces a relative clause 'who/that'; از تخمه او is a possessive phrase 'from his seed/lineage' (تخمه = offspring/stock) and بزیست is past of زیستن 'lived/was born' (3rd‑singular), so the clause means 'that person who came from his seed'.
segments.s302-g35: ز بهر is a poetic prepositional phrase 'for the sake of' (ز = 'because of/for', بهر = 'for'); گرانمایه 'precious/esteemed' modifies فرزند 'son/descendant'. خویش is expressed as خوی + ش (خوی = kinship, ش = enclitic 'his') forming 'his own'.
segments.s302-g36: A simple nominal list: بزرگان 'the great ones/elders', و 'and', خویشان 'kinsmen/relatives', و 'and', پیوند خویش 'his bonds/kinship ties' (پیوند = bond/link, followed by خوی + ش 'his'). The final خویش again carries the possessive suffix -ش.
segments.s302-g37: خروشی 'a roar/clamour' is the subject; بر آمد is a compound verb meaning 'arose' (بر functions as a prefix/particle combining with آمد 'came'); تو گفتی is 'you said' (2nd‑person past), and که introduces the content clause 'that'; ابر 'cloud' is the subject of the following clause. Note: گفتی here is the past 2nd singular verb 'you said'.
segments.s302-g38: همی is a continuous/adverbial particle 'kept/continually', خون 'blood' is the object, چکاند is past (3rd‑singular) 'let drip / made to drip', ز 'from' marks source, چشم 'eye', and هژبر is a proper name — together: 'the cloud was continually making blood drip from Hezbar's eye.'
segments.s302-g39: همی 'still/continually' frames the clause; بودش is the past form of بودن with the enclitic -ش (here 'he/it was/he remained'), اندر 'in/within', بخارا the place name Bukhara, and درنگ 'delay/tarry' (noun) — the clause says there was/did remain a delay in Bukhara (i.e. he lingered).
segments.s302-g40: همی 'still' again; خواست is past of خواستن 'he wished/wanted'; کایند is an archaic/poetic form meaning roughly 'that they come' or an old subjunctive/optative marker referring to 'they (should) come'; شیران 'lions' is a metaphor for brave men/heroes; به جنگ 'into battle' (به = to/into, جنگ = battle).
segments.s302-g9: بزرگیش is بزرگ + possessive -ش, ‘his greatness’; با + noun is a prepositional phrase ‘with/firm to’. پیوسته is a past/participial/adjectival form ‘joined/connected’ placed after the prepositional phrase. باد here is the optative particle ‘may (it be)’, so the whole clause is a wish: “May his greatness be joined to the mountain.”
segments.s302-g10: دل is the affected noun ‘heart(s)’; بدسگالان او is a possessive/genitive sequence ‘his بدسگالان’ (here: ill‑willed/ill‑omened people or ill‑wishers). خسته is the predicate adjective ‘weary/tired’. باد again is the optative ‘may’: the phrase means literally ‘May the hearts of his ill‑wishers be weary.’
segments.s302-g11: رسیدم is first‑person singular past ‘I arrived/reached’ (the -م marks ‘I’). ز ایران is the ablative ‘from Iran’. به + noun marks destination ‘to/into the ...’; ریگ فرب is a noun + postpositive adjective ‘sand (that is) fat/rich’. The clause reads ‘I reached the rich sands from Iran.’
segments.s302-g12: سه qualifies جنگ گران to give ‘three heavy/serious battles’. کرده شد is a passive/perfective idiom (کرده + شد) meaning ‘were carried out / were done’. در سه شب is the time phrase ‘in three nights’. So: ‘Three heavy battles were carried out in three nights.’
segments.s302-g13: شمار سواران افراسیاب is a noun phrase meaning 'the number of Afrasiab's riders.' شمار is the head noun 'number/count,' followed by a genitive chain (سوارانِ افراسیاب) expressing 'the horsemen of Afrasiab.' Classical/poetic Persian often omits the ezafe marker in writing, so the possession/association is shown by word order.
segments.s302-g14: نبیند is the 3rd-person singular negative present/subjunctive form of دیدن used here as a general negative ('does not see' / 'never sees'), placed before its subject خردمند (inverted word order typical of poetry). هرگز 'never' and the prepositional phrase به خواب 'in a dream' are adverbials modifying the verb; the direct object (the number of horsemen) is understood from the previous segment and thus omitted.
segments.s302-g15: بریده is a past participle 'cut off' or 'severed' used attributively before سر 'head' (so 'severed head'). چو here functions as 'as/amounting to' introducing the numeral ('as many as three hundred'), and نامدار is an adjective 'renowned' modifying سر. Together they mean 'severed, as many as three hundred renowned heads.'
segments.s302-g16: فرستادم is the 1st-person singular past tense 'I sent,' with its object (the severed heads) expressed in the previous segment; اینک is an adverb ('now' / 'behold') inserted after the verb for emphasis; بر شهریار is the prepositional phrase 'to the king' (بر here = 'toward/to'). The clause thus reads 'I sent (them) now to the king.'
segments.s302-g121: به سغد اندرون = a prepositional locative phrase: به + place (سغد) + اندرون (archaic/poetic 'inside/within'), so 'within Sogdiana'. بود is the past copula 'was' placed before the nominal predicate. یک ماه شاه is a poetic apposition: 'یک ماه' ('a moon', used metaphorically for a beautiful/glorious person) modifies شاه ('king'), yielding 'a king who is a moon among kings.' Persian poetry permits this inverted order (copula before the predicate and the epithet following it).
segments.s302-g122: همه سغد is the subject, 'all Sogdiana / all the Sogdians'. شد is the intransitive verb 'became'. شاه را نیک‌خواه: را marks شاه as the target/definite referent and نیک‌خواه functions as predicate noun/adjective 'well‑wisher' (i.e. 'well‑wishing toward the king'). The clause thus means 'all Sogdians became well‑wishers of the king.'
segments.s302-g123: سپه را — the object marked with را: 'the army'. درم داد — literal 'gave dirhams' (داد = past 'gave'). و آسوده کرد — 'and made/caused (them) to be at ease' (آسوده = 'at peace/relieved', کرد = causative/making verb). The sequence (object first, then verbs) is normal in Persian narrative.
segments.s302-g124: همی is an archaic/poetic particle denoting continued or habitual action ('ever/then/continually'). جست is the past form of 'to leap/spring' (here 'he leapt/rushed'). هنگام روز نبرد is a temporal phrase 'at the time/daylight of battle' (هنگام = 'at the time of', روز = 'day/time', نبرد = 'battle'), so the clause describes a leap/rush at the moment of battle.
segments.s302-g125: هر آن کس که بود is a relative clause: 'every/any person who was...' (که + بود = 'who was'). از در کارزار is a locative/instrumental phrase 'from/on/at the field of battle' (از = 'from/of', در here 'in/at' the war‑field, کارزار = 'battle/field'). Together: 'whoever was in the battlefield...' (i.e. whoever had experience in battle).
segments.s302-g126: بدانست is the past simple 'knew/understood'. It takes the following nouns as its objects: نیرنگ و بند حصار — 'stratagem/trick and the bonds/works of fortification'. Here نیرنگ = 'stratagem, trick', بند with حصار forms a compound meaning something like 'bond/ties or siege‑works of the fortress' (i.e. fortification/siegecraft). The construction lists the two skills the battlefield‑veterans knew.
segments.s302-g127: بیاورد is a past tense verb 'he brought' (poetic/imperfective narrative). و با خویشتن یار کرد: with خویشتن ('with himself') + یار کرد ('made [them] companions/turned [them] into allies'), where یار is the object/predicate 'companion' and کرد 'made/caused'. Word order places the prepositional phrase before the predicate verb, common in Persian.
segments.s302-g128: سر بدکنش is a possessive/genitive grouping: سر ('head') of بدکنش ('the ill‑doer' or 'wrongdoer'). پر ز تیمار کرد is a causative clause: پر ('full') + ز (poetic 'of/from') + تیمار ('care/worry') + کرد ('made'). Altogether: 'he made the wrongdoer's head full of care' = 'he filled the wrongdoer's head with worry.' Note the poetic use of ز for 'of' and پر for 'full (of)'.
segments.s302-g161: دلیران (subject) comes first: 'the brave'. به دژها is a directional prepositional phrase ('toward/to the fortresses'). نهادند is the past tense, third‑person plural of نهادن ('set/placed') and here forms the idiom نهادنِ روی = 'to set one's face toward / to turn toward'. روی is the noun 'face' used idiomatically after نهادند to mark the direction of attention ('turned their faces').
segments.s302-g162: به هر دژ که بودی is a relative clause: به هر دژ = 'to/at each fortress' and که بودی ('that/which was') uses the poetical past form بودی for 'was/stood there'. The verbless noun phrase یکی جنگجوی follows as the predicative: 'one warrior' — the clause means 'at every fortress that stood there there was one warrior.'
segments.s302-g163: This clause has an inverted classical order: شدی is the past of شدن ('became') used here; باره functions adverbially ('on that occasion' / 'again' in sense of 'then') and دژ هم = 'the fortress also'. آنگاه 'then' reinforces the temporal sense; پست is the predicate adjective 'low/laid low'. Together: 'the fortress, too, then became low/was leveled.'
segments.s302-g164: نماندی is the past form of ماندن with the negative prefix ن‑, so 'did not remain / was not left.' The nouns در ('door'), بام ('roof') and جای ('place') are linked by و (and); نشست is used as a noun/infinitive 'a place to sit' (جایِ نشست = 'place to sit'). The whole clause says that no door, roof, or seat remained.
segments.s302-g165: A simple list of beings/items: غلام 'slave', پرستنده 'worshipper/devotee', چارپای 'four‑footed animal / beast of burden'. The conjunction و links them; they are the items that, as the next segment states, did not remain.
segments.s302-g166: نماندی again is the negative past 'there did not remain.' بد و نیک ('bad and good') modifies چیزی ('anything / a single thing'), so 'not a thing, bad or good, was left.' به جای is an idiom meaning 'in its place' or 'remaining in place.' The clause denies that anything (neither good nor bad) was left behind.
segments.s302-g167: بر این‌گونه = 'in this manner / thus' (بر marking the manner). فرسنگ بر صد گذشت is a classical measure phrase: فرسنگ = 'parasang' and بر صد ('to the amount of a hundred') with گذشت (past of گذشتن) 'passed / went by.' The verb گذشت governs the temporal/measure phrase: 'a hundred parasangs passed in this way.'
segments.s302-g168: A series of negations describing the aftermath: نه ... نه ... 'neither ... nor ...'. دژ ماند آباد = 'no fortress remained inhabited' (ماند past 'remained', آباد predicative 'inhabited/prosperous'). جایی نه دشت continues the negation: 'nor any place, nor any plain.' Word order places the negator نه before the items for emphasis; ماند is the main verb 'remained.'
segments.s302-g129: وز آن جایگه گردن افراخته is a compact prepositional + participial phrase. وز is a poetic variant/usage equivalent to 'از' ('from' / 'then') introducing the place; آن جایگه 'that place' is its object. گردن افراخته is a participial adjective phrase (گردن 'neck/face' + افراخته 'raised') describing the (omitted) subject — literally 'having raised his head'. The subject (he/they) is omitted as is common in Persian narrative.
segments.s302-g130: کمر بسته و جنگ را ساخته contains two coordinated predicates. کمر بسته is an idiomatic participial phrase meaning 'having girded (his) loins' (کمر 'waist / girdle' + بسته 'bound/tied' used as past participle). و 'and' links to the main verb ساخته, which here is a past-predicative/past participle functioning as the verb 'prepared / made' with جنگ را marked by را as the definite/definite-object marker ('prepared the war' = 'prepared for battle'). The subject remains implicit.
segments.s302-g131: ز سغد کشانی سپه بر گرفت begins with ز 'from' (poetic), followed by سغد marking origin ('Sogd' / 'Sogdiana'). کشانی here is a verbal/adjectival element attached to سغد forming an agent/descriptor (roughly 'from Sogd' / 'Sogdian-...'). سپه 'army' appears before the verb because Persian often places the object/affected noun before the verb in poetic prose; بر گرفت is the compound verb (بر + گرفت, past of گرفتن) meaning 'lifted/raised' or 'mustered' — 'to raise an army.'
segments.s302-g132: جهانی در او مانده اندر شگفت is a verbless/stative construction where جهانی 'the whole world' is the topical subject. در او مانده is literally 'remained in/at him' (در as locative, او 'him', مانده past participle/stative 'remained/was left'), and اندر شگفت (archaic order) means 'in wonder/amazement.' The construction thus means 'the whole world remained in astonishment at him.' Note the archaic preposition اندر and the stative use of مانده instead of a finite auxiliary.
segments.s302-g133: خبر شد به ترکان که آمد سپاه is an impersonal reporting clause: خبر شد literally 'news happened' = 'it became known / word came'. به ترکان 'to the Turks' marks the experiencers/recipients. که introduces the subordinate content clause; آمد سپاه (inverted order) reports the content: 'the army came.' The past tense آمد indicates a completed event.
segments.s302-g134: جهانجوی کیخسرو کینه‌خواه is a noun phrase in apposition to سپاه: Kiḵsrow's army is described by two epithets. جهانجوی 'world-seeking' (epithet) and کینه‌خواه 'vengeful/avenger' flank the proper name کیخسرو; the whole string modifies/identifies the army. Word order is epithet + name + epithet — a common poetic stacking of modifiers.
segments.s302-g135: همه سوی دژها نهادند روی has the pattern 'everyone toward the fortresses set (their) face(s)'. همه سوی دژها is a prepositional/ directional phrase 'all/together toward the fortresses'. نهادند is the past tense of نهادن 'they placed/set', and روی 'face' is the noun object: together the phrase means 'they turned their faces toward the citadels' (i.e., they headed there). The subject 'they' is explicit in the verb ending -ند.
segments.s302-g136: جهان شد پر از جنبش و گفت و گوی is a copular/resultative clause: جهان شد 'the world became' + پر از 'full of' + a coordinated noun phrase جنبش و گفت و گوی ('stir/movement and talk/conversation'). شد is the past of شدن 'to become', and پر از marks the content of that fullness. گفت و گوی is the familiar compound meaning 'talk, conversation' (گفت 'speech/talk' + گوی 'saying').
segments.s302-g105: سپاهی is a collective noun meaning 'an army/a force' placed at the clause start as the thing sent. به سوی is a two-word prepositional expression meaning 'toward(s)'; بیابان سترگ is the noun phrase 'great/desert wilderness' with the adjective سترگ following the noun in poetic word order.
segments.s302-g106: فرستاد is simple past 3rd-singular 'he sent' (the subject is contextually the king). سالار ایشان طورگ is an appositive phrase: سالار 'commander' + ایشان 'their' (archaic 3rd-plural possessive) followed by طورگ in apposition (a proper name). The whole phrase identifies the leader of the sent force.
segments.s302-g107: پذیرفت is past 3rd-singular 'accepted' (here applied distributively). ز این 'from/of these' gives source; هر یکی 'each one' is the distributive subject phrase (placed after the verb in poetic order). جنگ شاه is 'the king's war/campaign' (noun followed by possessor without an explicit ezāfe in older poetic style).
segments.s302-g108: که introduces a subordinate clause of purpose/result 'so that/that'. بر نامداران uses بر in the sense 'against' (i.e. in opposition to) with نامداران 'the famed/renowned ones' (adjective used substantively). ببندند is the present-subjunctive 3rd-plural of بستن 'to close/block' used here for purpose ('they should block'); راه is the direct object 'the road/way'.
segments.s302-g109: جهاندار is an epithet 'world-ruler/sovereign' placed before the name; کیخسرو is the personal name. آن خوار داشت is a predicate: آن 'that', خوار 'despised/worthless', and داشت (past of داشتن) functions as 'he held/considered' — so 'he considered that one worthless.'
segments.s302-g110: خرد را places 'wisdom' as a definite object (را marks definiteness/emphasis). به اندیشه سالار داشت uses به 'to/in' with اندیشه 'thought/judgment' and سالار 'the commander' to say he 'had/placed wisdom in the commander's judgment' — i.e. he entrusted or relied on the commander's counsel; داشت is again past 'had/kept/entrusted'.
segments.s302-g111: سپاهی 'the army/forces' is modified by the relative clause introduced by که 'which/that'. از indicates origin 'from'; بردع (here a place-name in context) and اردبیل (a known city) are the sources. (Note: بردع is listed with non-place senses in the provided lexicon, so its sense here is a proper-name/place use.)
segments.s302-g112: بیامد is past 'came' (the troops arrived). بفرمود is an honorific/past verb 'he commanded/said' (here 'he ordered'). تا + خیل خیل is an idiomatic adverbial use: تا 'so/that' with reduplicated خیل 'host' meaning 'host after host' or 'in great crowds', indicating the manner/sequence of their coming.
segments.s302-g137: "به لشکر" is a prepositional phrase (to the army) placed at the start as the indirect object; "چنین گفت" is the past tense verb phrase 'said thus/so' (گفت = said) and comes before the subject in this poetic word order; "پس شهریار" places the subject 'then the king' at the end for emphasis, with پس meaning 'then'. Overall: 'To the army thus said then the king.'
segments.s302-g138: که introduces a reported clause ('that'); امروز = 'today'; به گونه = an idiomatic prepositional phrase 'in such a manner / in this way' (به + گونه 'manner'); شد is past tense 'became/was' or 'happened' (here 'took [this] form'); کارزار = 'battle' or 'battlefield'. The clause literally: 'that today in such a manner became the battle' → 'that today the battle has taken this form.'
segments.s302-g139: ز ترکان = 'as for/from the Turks' (ز = 'from/of' in poetic usage); هر آن کس که = 'whoever' or 'anyone who' (هر + آن + کس + که introduces a relative clause); فرمان کند = 'gives an order' or 'issues a command' (کند here is the present/habitual form of کردن used in a relative clause). The whole segment sets up 'whoever among the Turks commands...'.
segments.s302-g140: دل از جنگ جستن = a verbal-nominal phrase where دل (heart) + از جنگ + جستن (the infinitive 'to seek/try') expresses 'attempting to withdraw the heart from the fight' or 'trying to avoid battle'; پشیمان کند uses کند ('make' / causative sense here) with پشیمان ('regretful') to mean 'make him regret'. Grammatically, the infinitive phrase is the content that the subject (the one who commands) causes to be regretted: 'make [him] regret having tried to avoid battle.'
segments.s302-g141: مسازید = negative imperative (plural) 'do not make/raise' (here 'do not start'); جنگ is the object 'war'; و links clauses; مریزید = negative imperative (plural) 'do not spill' (here 'do not shed'); خون = 'blood' (object). Verbs precede their objects in this poetic ordering.
segments.s302-g142: مباشید = negative imperative (plural) 'do not be'; کس را uses را as the definite/accusative marker with کس ('anyone' / 'a person') as the object; به بد رهنمون = 'a bad guide' (به + بد modifying رهنمون). Literal order: 'Do not be to anyone a bad guide' → idiomatically 'do not mislead anyone / do not be a bad guide.'
segments.s302-g143: وگر = conditional 'if' (or 'but if'); جنگ جوید = 'seeks war' (جوید is present/subjunctive of جستن 'to seek'); کسی = 'anyone/person'; با سپاه = 'with the army/host' (با = 'with', سپاه = 'army'). The clause means 'If anyone seeks battle with the host...'.
segments.s302-g144: دل کینه دارش = 'his heart full of hate' where کینه‌دار is an adjectival compound 'hate-bearing' and -ش is the third-person possessive suffix ('his'); نیاید is negative optative/subjunctive 'should not come/shall not go'; به راه = 'on the road' or 'on the march'. Together: 'a man whose heart harbors hatred should not come on the march' i.e. do not let someone with a hateful heart go with the expedition.
segments.s302-g89: This clause is a simple past action: Subject (implied 'he') + object + indirect object + verb. گرانمایه (precious) qualifies گنجی (treasure); به marks the recipient (درویش), and داد is the past-tense verb 'gave.' Word order is adjective + noun + prepositional recipient + verb, typical of classical Persian narrative.
segments.s302-g90: کسی را introduces an indefinite/definite person as object/topic (را = object marker/emphatic). کز is the relative particle 'who/whom/from whom' linking to the clause that follows. شاد and بد are contrasted nouns/adjectives 'joy'/'harm' (poetic compression), and بیش داد is a compound meaning 'gave more' — together the phrase is a compressed comparative: 'he did (gave) more bad/harm than joy to him.' The structure is poetic inversion: emotion words before the verb, and بیش ('more') + داد ('gave') expresses the comparative outcome.
segments.s302-g91: وز here is the poetic contraction of و + ز ('and from'), introducing movement away from the previous place. آنجا is the place 'there'; بیامد is the past verb 'he came'; سوی + شهر + سغد = 'toward the city of Sogd.' Word order places the direction phrase after the verb, as common in classical Persian.
segments.s302-g92: یکی is an indefinite 'a certain / one'; نو جهان is a poetic compound meaning 'new life' or 'new being' (جهان used as 'life/existence'); دید is the past 'saw / beheld.' رسته ز is a past participle 'sprung/freed from' with ز = 'from', and چغد names a small bird. The participle phrase follows the object, describing its origin.
segments.s302-g93: ببخشید is the past verb form meaning 'bestowed / gave' (here from بخشیدن 'to give/bestow'), with گنجی as the object. بر آن شهر is the prepositional phrase indicating the recipient ('upon that city'), and نیز adds 'also' (likewise). The verb precedes the prepositional recipient phrase, a normal ordering in this style.
segments.s302-g94: همی خواست is an idiomatic past/habitual expression 'he wished/was wanting' (همی marking continuity or emphasis). کآباد is an adjective 'flourishing/prosperous' used here as a predicative desiderative, and گردد is the subjunctive/passive-like 'should become' or 'be made.' به چیز ('by/with a thing') indicates the means (here: by the gift). Together they form an object clause of خواست: 'he wished that it should become prosperous by that thing.'
segments.s302-g95: به هر منزلی is a locative phrase 'at every station/halt' (به + هر + منزلی). زینهاری qualifies سوار: this adjective/noun before noun pattern yields 'a rider of warning' — i.e., a mounted herald bearing admonitions. The phrase groups location + qualifier + noun in classical descriptive order.
segments.s302-g96: همی آمدندی is an archaic iterative/third-person plural past 'they would come' or 'kept coming' (همی marking repeated action; آمدندی is plural past). بر شهریار uses بر with the object 'شهریار' to mean 'to/unto the king.' The plural verb agrees with the plural implied subject 'سوار' (riders) from the previous segment.
segments.s302-g81: همی بود is a continuous/past imperfect expression meaning 'it continued' or 'it kept being'. تا introduces a temporal clause 'until'. بر چه گردد جهان literally orders the elements 'on what (بر چه) the world (جهان) may turn (گردد)'; here گردد is a present/jussive-subjunctive sense of 'turn/become' used in the subordinate 'until' clause, so the phrase means 'until the world turns upon/comes to (that)'.
segments.s302-g82: بدین آشکارا is an adverbial phrase 'by/with this openness' (ب = by/with; دین here is orthographic for این 'this' — see note). چه دارد نهان is a rhetorical question: چه 'what', دارد 'has/possesses' (present), نهان 'hidden/secret' — literally 'what has hidden (i.e. what can be hidden?)', meaning 'what could remain hidden given this openness.'
segments.s302-g83: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'. کیخسرو is the subject (proper name) and آمد is the simple past 'came'. بر این روی آب is a locative phrase 'on/at this surface/side of the water' (بر = on/at, این = this, روی = surface/side, آب = water).
segments.s302-g84: از او دور شد is a compound intransitive idea 'he moved away from it' (از = from, او = it/him, دور = away/removed, شد = became/moved). خورد و آرام و خواب lists activities/states connected to the subject: خورد (past of خوردن) 'he ate', followed by nouns آرام 'rest/peace' and خواب 'sleep' understood as things he took/experienced. The conjunctions و simply join the items.
segments.s302-g85: سپه ('the army/host') is the subject; چون introduces 'when'. گذر کرد is a light verbal expression 'passed' (گذر = passage/passing, کرد used to form the action 'did pass'). زان سوی رود is a prepositional/locative phrase 'to/that side of the river' (زان = from/that, سوی = side/direction, رود = river).
segments.s302-g86: فرستاد is the simple past 'sent'. زان پس is a temporal adverbial 'thereafter/after that' (زان = from/that; پس = then/after). به هر کس درود is the indirect-object phrase 'a greeting to each person' (به = to, هر کس = every person, درود = greeting).
segments.s302-g87: کز این آمدن is a causal/relative phrase 'because of/from this coming' (کز = from/that which; این آمدن = this coming). کس means 'anyone' or 'no one' depending on negative context. مدارید here functions as an admonition 'do not be afraid' / 'do not fear' addressed to listeners (form with -ید is 2nd plural form); باک is the noun 'fear'. The whole clause means roughly 'Let no one fear because of this coming.' Note: the provided sense list for مدارید does not match its use here, so that entry is marked accordingly.
segments.s302-g88: بخواهید is an optative/imperative 'ask/seek/pray' (addressing the addressees), followed by ما را 'for us' (را marks the object). ز یزدان پاک means 'from the Pure God' or 'of the Holy God' (ز = from/of, یزدان = God/divinity, پاک = pure). The phrase asks listeners to pray to God on the speaker's behalf.
segments.s302-g177: A noun phrase: سراپرده is the head (literally 'inner curtain/inner chamber') followed by the title شهریار جهان ('king/sovereign of the world') in apposition/possessive relation; Persian often strings such nouns without an explicit ezāfe. Together they mean 'the sovereign of the world's inner tent/chamber.'
segments.s302-g178: کشیدند is perfect (past) 3rd person plural 'they drew/strewn/erected' — here used in the sense 'pitched/raised' (a tent). بر پیش is a prepositional phrase 'before/in front of,' followed by آب روان where روان is an adjective 'flowing/running' modifying آب 'water' (i.e. a running stream). The phrase as a whole: 'they pitched ... before the running water.'
segments.s302-g179: جهاندار is the subject 'the sovereign.' بر + تخت زرین is the prepositional phrase 'on the golden throne' (تخت = 'throne', زرین = 'golden'). نشست is the past-tense verb 'sat' (third-person).
segments.s302-g180: خود here is emphatic/reflexive 'himself' and is coordinated with نامداران ('the renowned/illustrious ones') by و ('and'). خسروپرست is a postpositive modifier meaning 'Khosrow-worshipping' or 'devotees of Khosrow' describing نامداران. The construction lists the sovereign himself together with these famed courtiers.
segments.s302-g181: شبی is an adverbial 'one night' (or 'during the night'). کرد is past 'made/did' and takes جشنی ('a feast') as its object. که introduces a subordinate clause 'that/until' and تا روز پاک means 'until the clear/pure day' (پاک here = 'clear/dawn-like').
segments.s302-g182: همی before the verb gives a classical continuous/intonational flavor (here contributing the sense 'indeed/so that' or iterative aspect). مرده 'the dead' is the subject; برخاست is past 'rose (up)'. از تیره خاک is a prepositional phrase 'from the dark/dusky earth' (تیره = 'dark/bleak', خاک = 'earth/dust'). Grammatically this completes the clause introduced by که/تا: the celebration continued so long that even the dead rose from the dark earth.
segments.s302-g17: This is a short nominal list of relations. The items are presented in parallel: برادر (relative/kinsman), بد (here a brief epithet placed after برادر; literally 'bad' but used as an attributive word in the list), و (and) links items, خوی ش (خوی + enclitic ش = 'his kin/kinship') and پیوند اوی (پیوند 'bond/ tie' + اوی, a possessive meaning 'his'). The possessive enclitic اوی attaches to پیوند, marking possession; word order is noun + possessive (typical in older/poetic Persian).
segments.s302-g18: گرامی here is an adjective/epithet meaning 'honored' or 'esteemed' placed before بزرگان ('the great ones') — the phrase means 'honored by/among the great'. The conjunction و links the following noun phrase فرزند اوی ('his child/son'), where اوی is the possessive enclitic 'his'. The construction uses juxtaposition rather than an explicit preposition to express the 'of/among' relation.
segments.s302-g19: وز is an archaic/poetic prepositional form (functioning like از 'from/of'); here it introduces a partitive phrase 'from those renowned ones'. نامداران is the plural 'the famed/renowned ones'. بسته دویست is a poetic phrasing meaning 'numbering two hundred' or 'two hundred bound/tied' — بسته is used here in the sense 'amounting to/constituting' (older usage).
segments.s302-g20: که introduces a restrictive/relative clause describing those two hundred ('of whom/that'). صد شیر با جنگ is an adverbial phrase 'a hundred lions in battle' (با جنگ = 'in battle' / 'in war'). هر یک ('each one') is the subject of the small clause and یکیست (= یکی است) is the predicate 'is one' — here idiomatically 'each of them equals a hundred lions' (i.e., each is as good as a hundred lions in battle).
segments.s302-g21: همه رزم is the subject phrase 'all the fighting' (جمع/collective). بر دشت خوارزم is a locative prepositional phrase 'on the plain of Khwarezm' (preposition بر + place noun). بود is the past copula 'was', so the clause states location and existence of the fighting in the past.
segments.s302-g22: ز (poetic 'from'/'by') + چرخ (here 'the wheel of heaven' = fate/heaven) forms an ablative/agent phrase 'by the wheel/heaven'. آفرین is used in a verbal/adjectival sense related to creation or ordaining (poetic: 'made/ordained by'), so ز چرخ آفرین = 'ordained by heaven/fate'. بر چنان رزم بود repeats/underscores 'there was such a battle' — بر before چنان is a locative/emphatic particle and بود is the copula 'was'.
segments.s302-g23: برفت is an archaic/poetic past verb meaning 'he went off' (the b‑ prefix appears in older forms). او 'he' is the subject; و ما 'and we' links the following action. از پس is a frozen adverbial phrase meaning 'from behind' or 'after (him)'; اندر دمان (اندر + دمان) is a locative phrase 'into the skirts/edge' (دمان = hem/skirt, i.e. edge of the land). Together: 'He went off and we, from behind, into the skirts followed/drew.'
segments.s302-g24: کشیدیم is a past plural verb 'we drew/ endured/held on' (here best rendered 'we endured / waited on'); تا introduces a temporal clause 'until'. بر چه گردد زمان is an idiomatic subordinate clause 'until time turns/changes to what' — i.e. 'until it becomes clear what time will bring/what will happen'. گردد is the present-subjunctive/future sense 'becomes / comes to pass'.
segments.s302-g145: شما را حلال است + infinitive: This is an old/dative-style construction meaning 'to you is permitted...'. 'شما را' functions like an experiencer/dative ('to you'), not as a direct object; 'حلال است' is a predicate meaning 'is lawful/permitted'. The following infinitive-noun 'خون ریختن' (a masdar/Verbal-noun) is the thing that is permitted: 'to shed/spill blood.'
segments.s302-g146: This phrase gives the place/extension of the permission. 'به هر جای تاراج' = 'in/at every place of plunder' (the preposition 'به' governs the locative), and the second infinitive 'آویختن' is coordinated with 'خون ریختن' by the preceding 'و' — so both actions are permitted. In this syntax 'به' scopes over the place phrase and the coordinated verb phrase.
segments.s302-g147: 'به ره' + (بر) = 'on the road/way'; here the clause concerns conduct while on the march. 'خورشها' (the stews/dishes) is the object/topic and 'مدارید' is an admonitive/jussive addressed to 'you' (pl.) meaning 'do not stint / be sparing'. 'تنگ' follows as a predicate/adverbial meaning 'scarce/tight' — together: 'on the road, do not make the stews scarce.'
segments.s302-g148: Two coordinated admonitions. The first verb 'مدارید' again functions as an exhortation ('do not hold/nurse' or 'do not be full of') with 'کین' ('hatred/vengeance') as its object. The second verb 'مسازید' is a prohibitive 'do not make' (here 'do not make/raise') and takes 'جنگ' ('war') as its object. The 'و' links the two prohibitions.
segments.s302-g149: 'خروشی بر آمد' is a verbal phrase meaning 'a cry/roar arose' (برآمد as the compound verb 'arose'). 'ز پیش سپاه' is a locative: 'from before/in front of the army' ('ز' = from, 'پیش' = before/in front of, 'سپاه' = army). Word order places the verbal phrase then the source phrase.
segments.s302-g150: 'که' introduces a relative/subordinate clause describing that clamor. Here 'گفتی' is used as the reported/uttered word (a nominal/statement sense: 'what was said'), and 'بدرد همی چرخ و ماه' means 'it causes pain/trouble to the sky and the moon' — 'بدرد' = 'to cause pain, to afflict', with 'همی' marking the ongoing/imperf. aspect. So the clause reports that the noise was (as you said) grievous to heaven and moon.
segments.s302-g151: 'سواران' is the subject ('horsemen'). 'به دژها' marks the direction/target ('toward the fortresses'). 'نهادند' is the past 3rd-plural of نهادن 'to set/put' and here combines with 'روی' to mean 'they set/turned their faces' — a common poetic way to say 'they turned toward' (i.e., they advanced or faced the fortresses).
segments.s302-g152: 'جهان شد پر از ...' is a copular/result construction: 'the world became full of ...'. 'پر از غلغل و گفتگوی' = 'full of uproar and conversation/talk' — 'شد' is 'became' and 'پر از' introduces the content that fills the world.
segments.s302-g97: از آن پس is a temporal adverbial phrase meaning “after that/thereafter.” چو introduces a subordinate temporal clause meaning “when.” آگاهی (literally “news/information”) is the semantic subject and آمد is a past-tense verb “came/reached”; به شاه marks the recipient with the preposition به (to). The word order is slightly poetic (verb before the subject phrase can occur in Ferdowsi).
segments.s302-g98: This is a coordinated prepositional/oblique phrase giving the content or source of the news: ز + noun lists items. The preposition ز (poetic variant of از) here functions like “from/concerning,” and و links the coordinated items (the first item is uncertain/possibly a name). The whole phrase completes the clause started before.
segments.s302-g99: که introduces a relative/subordinate clause (“which/who”) referring back to the preceding. آمد is the past verb “came”; به نزدیک او is a prepositional phrase “to/near him” (به + نزدیک + او); گلگله is the noun meaning an outcry/clamour (here the thing that ‘came’). In Ferdowsi the verb may appear earlier for poetic effect, but the constituent relations are the same.
segments.s302-g100: ابا لشکری functions as a phrase meaning “with an army/detachment” (ابا here is an archaic/poetic variant closely functioning like با ‘with’), where لشکری is the military noun. چώن introduces a simile “like/as,” followed by the name/epithet هژبر and the adjective/epithet یله “wild/unrestrained.” There is no copula; the whole phrase is an adjectival comparison modifying the army.
segments.s302-g101: که again introduces a subordinate/relative clause. از تخم marks origin (“from the seed/stock of…”); the following nouns appear to be personal/ethnic names listed as sources (تورست, پرکین, و درد). Because some of these are archaic/proper names, their identification is uncertain in the supplied sense inventory; syntactically this entire phrase gives lineage/origin.
segments.s302-g102: بجوید is the present/habitual verb “seeks” (classical present form), with همی as an iterative/continuous adverbial particle “continually/still.” روزگار is the object meaning “time/age” (here ‘the time/season’), and نبرد specifies ‘of battle’ (a genitive/attributive compound: ‘the time of battle’). The clause states an ongoing desire for the era of fighting.
segments.s302-g103: فرستاد is the past tense verb “he sent/dispatched.” بهری (from بهر) here denotes a portion or detachment (“a part/one unit”) and ز گردان means “from the ranks/divisions” (ز = from). به چاج gives the destination (“to Chaj,” with چاج a place or proper name). The phrase describes the dispatching of a troop element to a location.
segments.s302-g104: که introduces the purpose/relative clause “who/that (in order) to ….” جوید is present “seeks,” with همی marking ongoing/habitual action. تخت ترکان is a possessive/genitive construction “the throne of the Turks” (noun + genitive modifier), and و تاج coordinates an additional object “and the crown.” Together they state the aim of the dispatched force.
segments.s302-g49: همانا که از صد نماندست بیست is a condensed classical clause: همانا is an emphatic adverb 'truly/verily'; که introduces the content clause; از صد = 'out of a hundred'; نماندست is a past negative 'did not remain' (old/poetic fused form). The numeral بیست follows to indicate 'twenty' in relation to نماندست (i.e. 'of a hundred, twenty did not remain' / contextually understood as how many remain or are lacking).
segments.s302-g50: بر آن رفتگان بر بباید گریست: بر ... گریست is an impersonal/obligative construction meaning 'one must weep over ...'. بر before رفتگان is the preposition 'over/against' marking the object of mourning; آن رفتگان = 'those departed'; بباید (from بایست/باید) is the modal 'one ought/must' and گریست (infinitive/past-stem used here as noun/verb) means 'to weep/lament'.
segments.s302-g51: کنون ما دل از گنج و فرزند خویش = 'now our heart from treasure and (our) children': کنون 'now'; ما subject 'we/our' attached here to show possession/agent; دل 'heart' is the semantic subject/affected item; از marks separation 'from'; گنج و فرزند خویش 'treasure and our own offspring' (خویش realized as ش = possessive). Word order is typical Persian: temporal adverb → possessor → noun → 'from' phrase.
segments.s302-g52: گسستیم چندی ز پیوند خویش = 'we have been broken/separated for a while from our bond': گسستیم is perfect/past plural 'we parted / were severed' (first-person plural suffix); چندی here means 'for a while'; ز (poetic variant of از) 'from'; پیوند خویش 'our bond/kinship' (خوی + ش possessive). The clause gives duration and source of separation.
segments.s302-g53: بدان روی جیحون یکی رزمگاه = 'on that account / therefore, (at) the Jeyhun a single battlefield': بدان روی is an idiomatic causal phrase 'on that account' (ب here functions as a particle/prefix + دان/روی forming the idiom); جیحون names the river; یکی 'one/a single' modifies رزمگاه 'battlefield'. Word order places the locative/name before the counted noun for poetic emphasis.
segments.s302-g54: بکردیم زان پس که فرمود شاه = 'we made/did that, after which the king commanded': بکردیم (ب + کردیم) is past plural 'we made/did/established'; زان پس 'after that/thereupon'; که introduces the subordinate clause 'that the king said/commanded'; فرمود شاه 'the king commanded/ordered'. The sequence makes clear the battlefield was set up following the king's order.
segments.s302-g55: ز بی دانشی آنچه آمد به روی = 'from/through lack of knowledge, whatever came upon (you)': ز 'from/because of' (poetic), بی 'without', دانشی 'knowledge' = 'through ignorance'; آنچه 'that which/whatever' is the relative subject; آمد 'came/happened' (past); به روی here is idiomatic 'upon' (= 'befell'). The clause states cause (ignorance) and what occurred.
segments.s302-g56: تو دانی که شاهی و ما چاره‌جوی = 'you know that (you) are king, and we are seekers of remedy': تو دانی present 'you know'; که introduces the content clause; شاهی here denotes 'kingship / that you are king' (predicate nominal with omitted copula); و ما چاره‌جوی 'and we (are) chare-juv' — 'chare-joy' is an adjectival noun 'seeker of a remedy', again copula omitted in Persian.
segments.s302-g41: از آن پس چو گشت انجمن آنچه ماند is a temporal subordinate clause: از آن پس (a fixed phrase 'after that' — از = 'from', آن = 'that') + چو 'when' introduces the time clause. گشت is the past-tense verb 'became/was made' and انجمن (assembly) follows it by poetic inversion. آنچه ماند ('that which remained') is a relative/indefinite phrase ('what remained') that refers forward to the people who are then summoned.
segments.s302-g42: بزرگان برتر منش را بخواند is the main action: بخواند is a past third-person verb 'he called/summoned' (classical past form placed at end). بزرگان برتر منش is the definite object phrase 'the great ones of superior origin/lineage' (منش = origin/lineage) and را is the definite-object marker attaching to that phrase. Word order is typical for classical Persian: object phrase comes before the final verb.
segments.s302-g43: چو گشتند پر مایگان انجمن is again a temporal clause: چو 'when' + گشتند (past plural 'they became' / 'it became for them') with inversion that puts the predicate before the subject. پر here means 'full (of)' and mایگان denotes persons/creatures filling the place; انجمن (assembly) names the place that became full.
segments.s302-g44: ز لشکر هر آن کس که بد رای زن is a relative/restrictive clause that describes which people are in view: ز لشکر 'from the army' (z = poetic 'from'), هر آن کس که 'every one who...', and بد رای modifies رای ('an ill/unfavourable counsel'). زن here is the verb at the end of the relative clause meaning 'gave/uttered' (classical placement of the verb at clause end) — it characterizes those soldiers as the ones who voiced bad counsel.
segments.s302-g45: زبان بر گشادند بر شهریار: زبان 'tongue/speech' is the object that is 'opened' (a set phrase meaning 'they spoke/they raised their voices'). گشادند is a past plural verb 'they opened/loosed'; بر شهریار ('against the king') uses بر = 'against' and places the target after it. The clause thus means 'they began to speak against the king.'
segments.s302-g46: چو بیچاره شدشان دل از کارزار is a subordinate temporal/causal clause introduced by چو 'when'. بیچاره 'unfortunate' modifies the subject; شدشان is the past verb 'became' with the enclitic pronoun شان 'to/for them' (i.e. 'their hearts became afflicted'). دل 'heart' is the thing affected, and از کارزار indicates cause/source 'from the battle'.
segments.s302-g47: که از لشکر ما بزرگان که بود is a nominal-relative statement introduced by که (here as 'that'/'for'): از لشکر ما 'from our army' identifies the group; بزرگان 'the great ones' is the noun being described; the second که + بود ('who were') is a relative clause marking those great men as 'who were [in our ranks]'. The clause is stylistically elliptical, preparing the following verb گذشتند.
segments.s302-g48: گذشتند و ز ایشان دل ما شخود contains the main past verb گذشتند 'they passed/they departed' (past plural). و 'and' links to the next clause; ز ایشان 'from them' indicates cause or source; دل ما 'our heart' is the subject of the following verbless/predicative phrase; شخود is a nominal predicate ('self‑conceit' / 'boastfulness') so the clause reads literally 'and from them our heart [became] shakhud' — a poetic, condensed way to state the emotional effect.
segments.s302-g1: بفرمود is an honorific past tense ‘he commanded/said’. تا introduces a subordinate clause of purpose/result (‘so that’). پیش او is a locative/phrasing meaning ‘before him / in his presence’. شد here functions as an intransitive verb meaning ‘came to be / happened’ (classical usage equivalent to ‘arrived’). دبیر is the noun (subject of شد) ‘scribe/secretary’ and follows the predicate in the classical word order.
segments.s302-g2: بیاورد is the subordinate jussive/present form meaning ‘bring’ (here as the commanded action). The direct objects (قرطاس و مشک و عبیر) follow the verb and are linked by و ‘and’; قرطاس = paper/sheet, مشک = musk, عبیر = fragrance/perfume. The clause expresses what the scribe was to bring.
segments.s302-g3: نبشتند is past plural ‘they wrote’; classical Persian often places the verb before the object. نامه is the direct object ‘a letter’. به + کاووس شاه marks the indirect object/recipient ‘to King Kavus’. The whole clause = ‘they wrote a letter to King Kavus.’
segments.s302-g4: چنانچون = ‘as / just as’. سزا here means ‘desert / what is fitting’ (i.e., appropriate reward or manner). بود is the past copula ‘was’. زان = ‘from that / of that’ and رزمگاه = ‘battlefield’. The phrase means roughly ‘as was fitting from that battlefield’ — i.e. in the manner appropriate to that fight.
segments.s302-g5: سر نامه = ‘the head/heading of the letter’ (سر used as ‘top/heading’). کرد is the past tense ‘he made/placed’ (i.e. he put as a heading). از + نخست = ‘from the first / at the very beginning’. آفرین here is a noun ‘acclamation’ or exclamation (‘praise/“Hail!”’) and specifies what he placed at the head.
segments.s302-g6: ستایش = ‘praise’. سزای is the relational/genitive sense ‘that which is due to / worthy of’ (desert). جهان‌آفرین = ‘the world‑creator’ (the Creator). Together the noun phrase reads ‘praise due to the Creator of the World.’
segments.s302-g7: دگر here = ‘again / then’. گفت introduces direct speech (‘said’); in the manuscript list the provided senses do not include the simple past ‘said’, so that item is marked accordingly. شاه جهانبان من is a vocative/epithet chain: شاه = ‘king’, جهانبان = ‘protector/guardian of the world’ used as a title, and من functions as the possessive ‘my’ in this older usage (addressing the king as ‘my king, my world‑guardian’).
segments.s302-g8: پدروار is an adjective ‘fatherly / paternal’ used as an epithet. لرزیده is a perfect participle ‘having trembled’ / ‘trembled’. بر is the preposition ‘on/upon/for’ and جان من = ‘my life’ (جان = life/soul; من here marks possession ‘my’). The clause therefore describes someone who has ‘trembled for my life’ or ‘shown fear/concern on my behalf.’
segments.s302-g153: هر آن کس که is a fixed relative phrase meaning “whoever / every person who.” فرمان به جا آورد(ن) is an idiom: فرمان (order) + به جا آوردن (to carry out). آوردید here is an archaic/classical past form of آوردن used for the clause’s verb (“carried out / executed”); word order is determiner + relative + object phrase + verb.
segments.s302-g154: سپاه شهنشه is the subject phrase “the king’s army.” بدو is a dative pronoun meaning “to him.” ننگرید is an archaic negative/past-form of نگرِش/نگر with the negative prefix ن- (“did not look/regard”); the verb follows the dative pronoun, so literally “the king’s army to him did not look” → “paid him no heed.”
segments.s302-g155: هر آن کو is another classical-style relative phrase meaning “whoever” (کو here functions like a relative 'who'). برون شد is a compound meaning “went out / left”; شد is the past form of شدن used with the directional برون to mark departure. ز فرمان شاه = “from the king’s command” (ز = poetic/archaic 'from').
segments.s302-g156: سرانشان is the object (their leaders/heads) with the possessive suffix -شان. بریدند is simple past plural “they cut / they severed.” یکسر is an adverb meaning “entirely / altogether” and here modifies the scope (the whole host); سپاه is the noun ‘host/army.’ Word order places object(s) before the past verb in this poetry line.
segments.s302-g157: ز ترکان = “from / among the Turks” (ز used poetically). کس here is the indefinite/negative sense (“anyone/no one”) determined by the negative verb later; از بیم افراسیاب is a prepositional phrase “out of fear of Afrasiab,” where از بیم = “because of / from fear of.” The whole phrase sets up who acted and their motive.
segments.s302-g158: لب‌تشنه is a compound adjective/noun meaning “(one) with parched lips” and functions as the object affected. نگذاشتندی is an archaic past plural negative (“they did not allow / they did not leave”), with the negative prefix ن-. بر آب is a locative phrase “at/to the water” (بر + آب), so literal order: “they did not leave (anyone) parched-lipped at the water.”
segments.s302-g159: وگر = “and if / but if.” باز ماندی is a classical phrase meaning “remained behind” (باز + ماندن; باز here is a prefixed element indicating being left/behind in older usage). کسی ز این سپاه = “anyone from this army/host.” The clause sets a conditional: if anyone was left behind from the army.
segments.s302-g160: تن بی سرش is a noun phrase meaning “his body without a head” (تن = body, بی = without, سرش = his head, the possessive enclitic -ش attached to سر). یافتندی is the archaic past plural “they found.” به راه is a locative “on the road.” Word order: object phrase (body-without-head) + past verb + location.
segments.s302-g113: بیایند و بگذرند are 3rd-person plural present-subjunctive/jussive forms (classical literary use) expressing actions ordered or intended — here dependent on an earlier بفرمود. بر پیش او is a prepositional phrase: بر (preposition) + پیش او 'in his presence, before him' (پیش + pronoun). The two verbs are coordinated by و and share the same subject.
segments.s302-g114: A simple nominal coordination (رد و موبد و مرزبان) joined by و, followed by بشمرند, another 3rd-person plural subjunctive meaning 'to count/number'. The whole phrase is another coordinated request/order: 'and (let them) count ...'.
segments.s302-g115: برفتند is the past indicative 'they departed/they went'. سالارشان is a possessed noun 'their commander' (سالار + possessive suffixشان) and گستهم here is a proper name (Gostaham) in apposition to سالارشان; Persian permits noun + name without a copula.
segments.s302-g116: که introduces a relative clause modifying گستهم ('who/for that in...'); در جنگ شیران is a locative 'in the battle of the lions' (شیران here = 'brave men' metaphorically). نبودی is an archaic past form 'you/he was not' (past tense), followed by the predicate adjective دژم 'grim/sullen' — the adjective follows the verb in this older style.
segments.s302-g117: همان گفت is a terse narrative clause 'he said (then/thereupon)' — گفت here is the past-tense verb 'said' (the lemma senses provided do not include this verbal meaning). تا introduces a temporal/subordinating clause 'until/when', and لشکر نیمروز names the army/unit (لشکر + نیمروز, here Nimruz as a regional/army name).
segments.s302-g118: برفتند is again past 'they set out/they went'; با introduces accompaniment 'with', followed by the personal name رستم and the epithet/epithetic name نیوسوز attached to him. The whole is a simple clause 'the army set out with Rostam Neyusuz.'
segments.s302-g119: بفرمود is an honorific/polite past tense 'he commanded/said' (often used to report a ruler's orders). تا introduces a purposive/result clause here ('so that/in order that'), and بر هیونان مست is a prepositional phrase: بر + هیونان 'upon/on the animals' with مست ('intoxicated' or figuratively 'reckless/mad') qualifying the state in which they should sit.
segments.s302-g120: نشینند and گیرند are 3rd-person plural present-subjunctive/jussive verbs coordinated by و, continuing the ordered actions ('let them sit/mount and seize'). اسبان 'horses' is the object of گیرند; به دست is an adverbial phrase 'by hand' or 'with the hand' (به + دست).
segments.s302-g57: گر introduces a conditional ('if'). ایدون is an older verb-form meaning 'it came/appeared' used here impersonally ('it became known'). که marks the following subordinate clause. روشن بود is a past-state predicate ('was clear'), and رای شاه ('the king's counsel/plan') is the noun + possessor (classical order without an ezāfe); together the clause means 'if it came to be known that the king's counsel was clear.'
segments.s302-g58: از ایدر is a prepositional phrase 'from here' (از + ایدر 'here'). به چاچ اندر combines to indicate motion/toward location ('to/into Chach'); اندر is a classical locative particle 'in/into'. آرد is a third-person singular verb 'brings' (here understood as the result of the conditional: 'he would bring'), and سپاه is the object 'army' placed after the verb in this older poetic word order.
segments.s302-g59: چو here means 'when' (temporal). کیخسرو is the subject (proper name). آید is the present/future-tense verb 'comes.' به کین خواستن literally 'to want/seek vengeance' — به marks purpose and خواستن is the infinitive 'to seek/want'; together the phrase means 'comes to seek vengeance.'
segments.s302-g60: بباید is a deontic/modal verb meaning 'must/ought' and governs the following infinitive construction. تو را uses the topical/accusative marker را after the pronoun 'you' (object of the verb phrase), and لشکر آراستن is the verbal phrase 'to array/prepare an army' (آراستن = to array/equip). In older Persian the object phrase follows the modal verb, producing 'must you [obj] army array'.
segments.s302-g61: چو 'when' introduces a temporal clause. شاه is the subject. اندر این کار is a locative/idiomatic phrase 'in this affair.' فرمان برد here is a classical collocation: فرمان + برد(ن) meaning 'to take/assume command' or 'to give an order' depending on context; grammatically برد is the verb element attached to فرمان. This is an older, compact verbal phrase meaning 'took/assumed command in this business.'
segments.s302-g62: ز (poetic form of از) marks origin or separation 'from/of.' گلزریون is a proper name/group. نیز هم is emphatic 'also/too.' بگذرد is a third-person present/future form 'passes' or 'will pass' (here following the temporal clause: 'he will also pass by the Gulzarion'). The phrase as a whole means 'he will also pass by (or go beyond) the Gulzarion.'
segments.s302-g63: بباشد is an optative/subjunctive ('let it be' / 'may it be'). به آرام is a prepositional phrase 'in/with calm.' به بهشت gives the destination or state 'to/into paradise,' and گنگ is an adjective 'mute/muffled/indistinct' modifying بهشت; the sequence is classical/poetic (repeated به). The clause is a wish or stipulation: 'let it be calm, in a muffled paradise.'
segments.s302-g64: که introduces an explanatory clause ('for/that'). هم adds emphasis 'also/both.' جای means 'place,' and جنگست is a predicative compound ('is war' / 'is a place of war') — the suffix -ست copula is attached to جنگ. و joins the two predicates, and جای درنگ names the other function: 'a place of pause/delay.' So the clause explains why the previous wish is made: 'for it is both a place of battle and a place to tarry.'
segments.s302-g73: یکی جای بود آن به سان بهشت is an inverted, slightly archaic word order: 'یکی' (a certain) + 'جای' (place) + past copula 'بود' (there was). 'آن' functions as a demonstrative/locative ('that / there'), and the simile 'به سان بهشت' ('like paradise') follows, with the preposition-like phrase 'به سان' + noun. Together the phrase means 'there was a certain place that was like heaven.'
segments.s302-g74: This is a coordinate/descriptive clause about that place. 'گلش' = گل + ­ـش (possessive suffix) = 'its flowers.' What follows are two coordinated descriptive noun phrases joined by 'و' ('and'): 'مشک سارا بد' and 'زر خشت'. Grammatically the first group attributes a musk‑quality to the flowers (a poetic phrasing where a noun cluster indicates smell/quality) and the second names the building material as 'gold bricks' ('زر' + 'خشت'). Note: 'سارا' and 'بد' here behave as poetic/compound elements; 'بد' appears as a short past/copular or verbal element in poetry (likely a contracted/poetic form rather than the ordinary adjective 'bad').
segments.s302-g75: بدان جایگه is a locative expression meaning 'in that place' (here written as bound elements). 'شاد و خندان' are two coordinated predicative adjectives ('happy and smiling/cheerful') describing the state. 'بخفت' is a past-tense verb meaning 'slept' (classical/poetic past). The clause therefore reads 'In that place [they] slept happy and smiling,' with the subject inferred from context.
segments.s302-g76: This clause reports speech or report: 'تو گفتی' is the 2nd-person marker 'you' plus a verb of saying/report (here functioning as 'you said' or 'it is said (to you)'). 'که' is the complementizer 'that.' 'با ایمنی گشت جفت' is a short clause in which 'با ایمنی' ('with safety' / 'in safety') is an adverbial phrase, 'گشت' is the past of 'to become' ('became'), and 'جفت' is 'a pair' / 'mates,' so the sense is 'that the pair became/was made safe together.' Word order is typical of Persian report clauses: [report phrase] + که + [content clause].
segments.s302-g77: This clause has a simple verb structure: 'سپه' (host/army) is the subject, 'خواند' is the verb 'called/summoned' (here used transitively), and 'از هر سوی بی‌کران' is a prepositional phrase 'from every boundless direction.' Word order is Subject–Verb–(prepositional phrase), but in poetry such order may vary for rhythm.
segments.s302-g78: This is a noun phrase listing those summoned: 'بزرگان' (the great ones) followed by two coordinated epithets 'گردنکش و مهتران' ('the headstrong/rebellious and the chiefs/elders'). The coordination by 'و' joins the final two terms. 'مهتران' is a plural noun meaning leaders/elders/chiefs in classical usage.
segments.s302-g79: This is a poetic catalogue of delights: items are listed in parallel, joined by 'و' ('and'). 'می‌و گلشن' pairs wine (or fruit in some contexts) with gardens, 'بانگ چنگ و رباب' is 'the sound/cry of the harp and the rebab' (two musical instruments). Grammatically, the nouns are paratactically listed; 'بانگ' (sound/voice) governs the instruments named after it.
segments.s302-g80: A continuation of the catalogue: simple coordination of nouns with 'و' ('and'): 'گل' (flowers), 'سنبل' (hyacinth), 'رطل' (a unit/measure, here listed as a valued object or weight), and the proper name 'افراسیاب' (Afrasiab). The sequence is a loose apposition / enumeration typical of epic listing.
segments.s302-g169: چو is a temporal conjunction meaning “when” that introduces a past event. آورد is the past-tense verb (آوردن) placed before its subject in a classical/poetic order; here it means “arrived/reached” or literally “brought (it) to.” لشکر is the subject “the army,” and به گلزریون is the locative prepositional phrase “to Golzariun.”
segments.s302-g170: به هر سو is a phrase meaning “to/into every direction” (هر modifies سو). بگردید is the past plural of گشتن/گردیدن here meaning “they roamed/turned about/searched.” با رهنمون is an instrumental phrase “with a guide/guidance,” indicating how the searching was done. The whole clause is coordinated with the previous temporal clause.
segments.s302-g171: جهان is the subject “the world.” دید is the past verb (here functioning like “looked/appeared”). بر سان is a classical comparative expression meaning “as/like,” where بر precedes the simile marker سان. باغ بهار is a noun phrase “garden of spring” (i.e. a spring garden) that is the basis of the comparison.
segments.s302-g172: This is a paratactic list of places (در here functions as “court/house/royal court” in poetic usage) joined by و: “در و دشت و کوه و زمین.” پرنگار is a postpositive adjective meaning “richly ornamented” or “highly decorated” that predicates those places (the copula is omitted because it is supplied by the prior verb دید).
segments.s302-g173: همه is the universal quantifier “all.” کوه نخچیر is a noun phrase meaning “mountains of game” or idiomatically “mountains full of game/prey.” The coordination و هامون درخت parallels that structure: هامون (plain) + درخت (trees), i.e. “plains full of trees.” There is no explicit copula because the describing verb earlier (دید) applies to these items.
segments.s302-g174: جهان restates the scene “the world.” از introduces a source/origin “from/out of,” and در here means “door/household” (poetic for dwelling). مردم نیک‌بخت is a genitive group “people (who are) fortunate,” so از در مردم نیک‌بخت = “from the houses/doors of fortunate people” (i.e. the world was full around the dwellings of lucky people).
segments.s302-g175: طلایه is the noun “vanguard/forefront” and فرستاد is the past verb “sent.” The conjunction و links a second object: کارآگهان (کارآگه in plural/collective form) means “those who are informed” — here “scouts/spotters/intelligence men.” Both nouns are objects of فرستاد.
segments.s302-g176: بدان is a fused locative/complement meaning “to there/thereto” (built from the preposition ب- + a demonstrative element); it indicates where the vanguard and scouts were sent. تا introduces purpose “so that,” و نماند is the negative jussive/optative of ماندن meaning “may not remain / did not remain,” بدی is the object “evil/harm,” and در نهان is a prepositional phrase “in hiding/secretly.” Altogether the clause states the purpose: they were sent so that no evil would remain hidden.

sentences.s302-s33: They all agreed on this at once; no one proposed another opinion.
sentences.s302-s34: They all went together to Golzariyun; every eye was full of water and every heart full of blood.

sentences.s302-s36: They departed from that spot toward Gank; there was no place for much delay.
sentences.s302-s13: Upon this battlefield they said, “May there be praise”; each year it was paired with an auspicious star.
sentences.s302-s14: They set on the letter a seal of musk; after that it passed over the dry sand.
sentences.s302-s15: When Afrasiab reached that river, the Jihun, the water rushed by like a swift wind.
sentences.s302-s16: He came before the army of Qara Khan; he kept saying what each man had seen of the battle.
sentences.s302-s17: How greatly the commander of the Turks wept for that man who had sprung from his line.
sentences.s302-s18: For the sake of his precious son — his nobles and kinsmen and his family ties.

sentences.s302-s20: He still lingered in Bukhara; he wished that the lions (brave men) would come into battle.
sentences.s302-s5: May his greatness be joined to the mountain; may the hearts of his ill‑wishers be wearied.
sentences.s302-s6: I arrived from Iran to the rich sands; three heavy battles were fought in three nights.
sentences.s302-s7: A wise man never sees the number of Afrasiab's horsemen in a dream.
sentences.s302-s8: I have now sent to the king three hundred renowned severed heads.
sentences.s302-s61: Within Sogdiana there lived a king like a moon; all of Sogdiana became his well‑wishers.
sentences.s302-s62: He gave dirhams to the army and put them at ease; then he leapt into the fray at the time of the day's battle.
sentences.s302-s63: Anyone who had been on the field of battle knew stratagem and the bonds/works of fortification.
sentences.s302-s64: He brought (them) and made them his companions with himself; he filled the head of the wrongdoer with care/worry.
sentences.s302-s81: The brave turned their faces toward the fortresses; at every fortress that stood there there was a single warrior.
sentences.s302-s82: Then the fortress too became low; no door, roof, or place to sit remained.
sentences.s302-s83: No slave, worshipper, or beast of burden remained; not a single thing, good or bad, was left in its place.
sentences.s302-s84: Thus for a hundred parasangs they passed on — no fortress remained inhabited, nor any place, nor any plain.
sentences.s302-s65: From that place, having lifted his head, he girded his loins and prepared for battle.
sentences.s302-s66: From Sogd he mustered an army; the whole world was left in wonder at him.
sentences.s302-s67: News reached the Turks that the army of the world‑seeking, avenging Kay Khosrow had arrived.
sentences.s302-s68: Everyone turned toward the fortresses; the world became full of commotion and talk.
sentences.s302-s53: He sent a force toward the great wilderness; their commander was Torg.
sentences.s302-s54: Each of these accepted from the king the campaign, that they might block the road against the renowned.
sentences.s302-s55: The sovereign Kay Khosrow held that one in contempt; he entrusted wisdom to the commander's judgment.
sentences.s302-s56: The troops that came from Bardah and Ardabil he ordered to come in host upon host.
sentences.s302-s69: Then the king said to the army: "Today the battle has taken this form,"
sentences.s302-s70: As for the Turks, whoever issues a command — if any man bids retreat from the fight — make him rue having tried to avoid battle.
sentences.s302-s71: Do not start a war and do not spill blood; do not be a bad guide to anyone.
sentences.s302-s72: And if anyone seeks battle with the host, a man whose heart is full of hatred should not go on the march.
sentences.s302-s45: He gave a precious treasure to a dervish — a man to whom he had done more harm than good.
sentences.s302-s46: And from there he came toward the city of Sogd; he beheld a newborn life sprung from a choghd (a small songbird).
sentences.s302-s47: He bestowed treasure on that city as well; he wished that it might become flourishing through that gift.
sentences.s302-s48: At every station a rider bearing admonitions would come to the monarch.
sentences.s302-s41: It lasted until the world turned; with such openness, what could remain hidden?
sentences.s302-s42: When Kay Khosrow came to this water's edge, he moved away from it and ate, rested, and slept.
sentences.s302-s43: When the host passed to that side of the river, it then sent a greeting to every person.
sentences.s302-s44: Let no one be afraid of this coming; pray (ask) for us of the Pure God.
sentences.s302-s89: They pitched the sovereign of the world's inner tent before the running water.
sentences.s302-s90: The sovereign sat on the golden throne — himself and the renowned Khosrow-worshippers.
sentences.s302-s91: One night he held a feast that lasted until the clear day; even the dead rose from the dark earth.
sentences.s302-s9: His brothers, kinsmen, and family-bonds — honored among the great ones, and his son.
sentences.s302-s10: And from those renowned men there were two hundred bound, each of whom in battle is the equal of a hundred lions.
sentences.s302-s11: All the fighting was on the plain of Khwarezm; by the wheel of heaven such a battle it was.
sentences.s302-s12: He departed, and we followed after him into the skirts; we endured until time would turn to see what would happen.
sentences.s302-s73: You are permitted to spill blood and to hang (the victims) wherever you plunder.
sentences.s302-s74: On the road, do not stint the stews — do not nurse hatred, and do not make war.
sentences.s302-s75: A clamor rose in front of the army, which, as you said, pains the sky and the moon.
sentences.s302-s76: The horsemen turned their faces toward the fortresses; the world became full of clamor and talk.
sentences.s302-s49: After that, when news reached the king concerning Gанг, Afrasiab, and the army.
sentences.s302-s50: For whom a clamour came near him, with an army like Hēzbar Yele.
sentences.s302-s51: who (is) of the stock of Tur — Perkin and Dard — and constantly seeks the times of battle.
sentences.s302-s52: He sent a detachment of the troops to Chaj, to seek always the Turks’ throne and crown.
sentences.s302-s25: Truly, of a hundred not twenty remained; one must weep for those who have gone.
sentences.s302-s26: Now our heart is torn from our treasure and our children; for a while we have been severed from our bonds of kinship.
